name: Coordinated Omission

x.defectClass: Systematically under-reported tail latency in load tests and benchmarks.
x.oracle: A correct latency model that counts the latency every request *should* have experienced, including requests delayed or never sent during a stall.
x.automationLevel: High — corrected tools (HdrHistogram, wrk2, Gatling) compensate automatically.
blindSpots: Average and even p99 can look fine while real tail latency is orders of magnitude worse
Closed-model load generators hide it by design
x.cost: Low — mostly a matter of using corrected tools and open-model load.

def: A pervasive benchmarking error where a load generator that waits for slow responses stops issuing requests during a stall, so the latency of the requests it "omitted" is never recorded — drastically understating tail latency.
problem: Closed-loop load tests that send the next request only after the previous returns hide exactly the latency spikes that hurt users.
mechanism: When the system stalls, a coordinated (synchronous) generator pauses too, omitting the requests that should have been sent — and their long waits. Correction: use an open-model generator (fixed send rate) or back-fill the missing samples (e.g. HdrHistogram / wrk2) so the recorded distribution reflects what users would have seen.
benefits: Naming and correcting it reveals true tail latency
Cheap to fix with the right tools
limits: Awareness problem more than a technique
Correction assumes an intended request rate
Does not fix the underlying latency, only the measurement
failures: Reporting p99 from a closed-model tool as if it were real
Trusting averages that mask multi-second stalls
whenNot: n/a — always account for it when measuring latency under load
myths: "Our p99 is 5 ms" — under coordinated omission the true p99 can be seconds
